coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of color rendering index of stable lead-free perovskite WLEDs through incorporating green carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wenfeng
Li, Xue
Li, Bo
Wang, Caizheng
Zhao, Ying
Zu, Xiaotao
Zhou, Weilie
Xiang, Xia
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the carbon quantum dots@Cs2NaInCl6 (CDs@CNC) composites are presented by a facile solution synthesis. The obtained CDs@CNC composites maintain the octahedral morphology of original CNC host and the characteristic functional groups of original CDs. As a result, the rare-earth-free CDs@CNC composites exhibit green light emission with good stability for UV irradiation, heat, and humidity. In addition, CDs were combined with ion-doped CNC to obtain multicolor phosphor and the corresponding white light emitting diode (WLED) devices were fabricated by coating composites on commercial UV chips. The color render index (CRI) of WLEDs increases significantly from 66.24 to 90.95 while the cold-warm white fluorescence can be flexibly tuned by the change of excitation wavelength. The combination of CDs and double perovskites provide a new route to design white light emission phosphors, which could lead to the fabrication of next generation high-quality lighting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cs2NaInCl6
Rare-earth-free
Color rendering index
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid misctext orcid primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12275047 ).

Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD patterns of Cs2NaInCl6 and CDs@CNC samples. The XRD pattern of CNC matches with reference [22] and the XRD pattern of CDs@CNC composite is highly similar to that of CNC, which indicates that the composite retains the cubic structure of the original CNC crystal. Here is no obvious XRD peak of CDs due to small amounts of CDs as well as the possible amorphous structure of synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), C, Cs, Na, In, and Cl elements were detected in the composite material by the EDS, where the C element can only come from CDs. The surface morphology and elemental distribution of composites were characterized by SEM and EDS mapping. The SEM image in Fig. 2 (c) shows the morphology of CDs@CNC composites and the image in Fig. S1 in Supporting Information (SI) shows the morphology of CNC hosts, indicating that CNC maintains the original octahedral morphology after the synthesis. Besides, the EDS mapping images in Fig. 2(d–g) show that the Cs, Na, and In elements are uniformly distributed in the visible octahedral shape. Fig. 2 (d) also shows a large amount of C element distributed outside octahedron due to the aggregation of uncombined CDs. However, the combined CDs cannot be clearly observed due to their dispersed distribution into the octahedron.This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12275047).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis scheme and photos of CDs@CNC samples.
(a) XRD patterns of CNC and CDs@CNC. (b) EDS spectrum of CDs@CNC. (c) SEM image of CDs@CNC. (d–g) EDS mapping images of C, Na, In, and Cs elements in CDs@CNC.

(a) TEM image of CDs@CNC composites and particle size distribution of CDs (inset). (b) High-resolution TEM image of the area in the dashed rectangle in Fig. 3 (a). (c) Raman spectra of CDs and CDs@CNC composites. (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs@CNC composites.
(a) Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of CNC, CDs solutions, and composites. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of samples (PLE and PL1 for CDs@CNC, PL2 for CDs solutions).
Time-resolved PL decay curves of CDs solutions and composites.

(a) PL intensity variation of CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites exposed to 240 min continuous UV irradiation. The inset shows the photos before and after UV irradiation. (b) PL intensity variation of CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites after 7 heating-cooling cycles. The inset shows the photos before and after 7 cycles. The PL spectra evolution of (c) CDs solutions and (d) CDs@CNC composites in air for 15 days.

PL spectra and corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates of composites: (a, d) CDs@CNC:0.01Sb&; 0.01Bi&1.2Mn with cold white emission, CDs@CNC:0.01Bi&; 1.2Mn with quasi white emission (b, e) and warm white emission (c, f). The insets are the corresponding WLED photos.
PL spectra of the CDs@CNC:Sb&Bi&Mn composites before and after combination with CDs.
Chromaticity coordinates, color temperatures and CRI (average of CRI, Ra) of cold white light, quasi white light and warm white light.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of color rendering index of stable lead-free perovskite WLEDs through incorporating green carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, 610054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610054, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Institute for Advanced Study, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Shenzhen, 518110, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Institute for Advanced Study, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Shenzhen 518110, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Advanced Materials Research Institute, University of New Orleans, New Orleans, LA, 70148, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Advanced Materials Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Advanced Materials Research Institute, University of New Orleans, New Orleans, Louisiana 70148, United States of America

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the carbon quantum dots@Cs2NaInCl6 (CDs@CNC) composites are presented by a facile solution synthesis. The obtained CDs@CNC composites maintain the octahedral morphology of original CNC host and the characteristic functional groups of original CDs. As a result, the rare-earth-free CDs@CNC composites exhibit green light emission with good stability for UV irradiation, heat, and humidity. In addition, CDs were combined with ion-doped CNC to obtain multicolor phosphor and the corresponding white light emitting diode (WLED) devices were fabricated by coating composites on commercial UV chips. The color render index (CRI) of WLEDs increases significantly from 66.24 to 90.95 while the cold-warm white fluorescence can be flexibly tuned by the change of excitation wavelength. The combination of CDs and double perovskites provide a new route to design white light emission phosphors, which could lead to the fabrication of next generation high-quality lighting devices.

ce:section.ce:para: White light emitting diodes (WLEDs) have become the key solid-state lighting source due to its energy conservation, long lifetime and environmental friendliness [1–4]. At present, the usual commercial WLED is the blue LED (InGaN/GaN chip) coated with yellow emission phosphor (YAG: Ce) [5–7]. However, its emitting color is affected by the changed amount of yellow phosphor and the degradation of blue LEDs. Fortunately, this can be mitigated by shifting the excitation wavelength from blue region to ultraviolet (UV) band [8,9]. Therefore, in recent years, phosphors mixed with UV chips have drawn much attention [10–12].

On the other hand, in order to better display the color of the object itself and create a comfortable atmosphere, WLEDs with a greater demand for high color rendering index (CRI) and an ultra-high CRI even above 90 is required in some occasions, such as museums, restaurants, jewelry stores and photo studios [13–15]. In general, the combination of multiple phosphors can achieve wide color gamut and improve the CRI of the corresponding WLED. For example, Yin and co-workers [16] reported a white-light emission with a high CRI of 81 by loading multi-color (blue, green and red) dyes onto metal organic frameworks. Bae et al. successfully prepared a WLED with ultra-high CRI of 93 by mixing four colors (blue, cyan, yellow and red) quantum dots according to the certain proportion [17]. Hence, multicolor fluorescence excited by UV chips is feasible to meet the requirements of next generation high-quality lighting. However, the multicomponent phosphors show the low luminescent efficiency due to self-absorption of photons and the different degradation rates of multiple components lead to the color change of WLED after a long-time operation [18,19].

In recent years, double perovskite structural Cs2AgInCl6 and Cs2NaInCl6 have become exciting single-component phosphors because of their excellent optical properties, e.g., direct band gap, simple preparation, environmental friendliness and air stability [20,21]. Tang’s group prepared the single-emitter-based phosphors by co-doping Na+ and Bi3+ into Cs2AgInCl6 to obtain highly efficient white-light-emitting [21]. Unfortunately, Cs2AgInCl6 based double perovskites show photo-degradation during continuous illumination with UV radiation due to the partial reduction of Ag + to Ag0 [22]. Cs2NaInCl6 (CNC) has weak light absorption because of its wider band gap than Cs2AgInCl6, which inhibits the photobleaching effect. Thus, CNC has recently attracted the interest for phosphor, and its optical performance has been improved by ion doping [23–25]. For example, Zhou et al. reported the Sb3+ and Bi3+ co-doped CNC, which presents the blue and yellow dual-color coordinated emission with high efficiency [20]. However, the WLEDs with CNC-based phosphors still suffer from a low CRI. Besides, doping is the dominated method to improve the optical performance of CNC, where most of the green emissions rely on the introduction of rare earth elements [26,27]. The commercialization of rare earth elements is limited by their high price and shortage. Therefore, it is important to find new methods to improve the CRI of CNC-based WLEDs without rare earth elements.

Designing composites is an effective strategy to improve optical properties [28,29]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) with excellent luminescent properties are ideal precursor candidates of composite luminescent materials since they are usually incorporated into a solid host [30–32]. For example, Zhai and co-workers successfully prepared the CDs dispersed in NaCl crystals with good thermal and chemical stability [33]. The CDs dispersed in barium chloride crystals (CDs@BaCl2) show a wide-spectrum solid-state green-emission and good thermal stability, and the CRI of fabricated WLEDs was improved by introducing CDs@BaCl2 [34]. Chen et al. successfully designed and prepared WLEDs with a high CRI by assembling blue-emitting CDs with yellow-emitting zero-dimensional perovskite (Cs2InCl5·H2O:Sb3+) [19], and the composites show a stable white light emission with a tunable color temperature. The CNC as another popular lead-free perovskite can achieve multi-color emissions by ion-doping, which is positive for high CRI. However, the three-dimensional double perovskite structure of CNC differs from the zero-dimensional perovskites [35–37], and it is unknown whether CDs can maintain their luminescence properties after the combination with double perovskites. At present, there is no report on improving the performance of three-dimensional double perovskite based WLED through incorporating CDs. Based on the above literatures, the combination of CNC with CDs is a promising approach to explore to improve the fluorescence performance of CNC.

In this work, the rare-earth-free CDs@Cs2NaInCl6 composite was successfully synthesized by a facile solution method. The as-synthesized composites demonstrate good stability against UV irradiation, heat and air, and the CRI of fabricated CNC-based WLEDs increases significantly from 66.24 to 90.95. In addition, the color temperature of the WLEDs can be easily tuned through changing the proportion of composite precursors and the excitation wavelength. This work provides a new strategy to design white light emission phosphors toward next generation high-quality lighting devices.

CsCl (99.99% metals basis), InCl3 (anhydrous, 99.99% metals basis), NaCl (99.99% metals basis), MnCl2 · 4H2O (99.99% metals basis), SbCl3 (99.9% metals basis), BiCl3 (99.9% metals basis), and citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, analytical reagent) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemicals were used as received with no further purification.

The preparation methods of CDs refer to the previous reports [38,39] with minor changes. In a typical synthesis, 2 g citric acid powder was fully ground and added into a dry and clean crucible. After that, the crucible was put into the oven and heated at 200 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid product was ground and dissolved in 300 mL deionized water and then centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was dialyzed with a molecular-weight cut-off (MWCO: 1000) membrane for 24 h. Finally, the brown CDs solution was obtained and subjected to lyophilization.
Synthesis of CDs@CNC, CDs@CNC:Bi,Mn & CDs@CNC:Sb,Bi,Mn.

The Cs2NaInCl6 were prepared using a method similar to the one reported by Saikia et al. [22] with minor changes. The CDs@CNC composites with different CDs contents were synthesized to investigate the effect concentration of CDs on the luminescence performance of composites. 1 mmol InCl3 (0.221 g) and 1 mmol NaCl (0.059 g) were dissolved in 5 mL HCl with constant stirring at 78 °C to prepare the precursor solution. 2 mmol CsCl (0.336 g) and different amounts of CDs (9 mg, 45 mg, 90 mg) were dissolved in 2 mL deionized water with fully stirring for 30 min, and then it was poured into precursor solution to form CDs@CNC precipitation. The reaction continued for another 5 min and then the mixed solution was cooled to room temperature. Finally, the obtained precipitation was washed with isopropyl alcohol and vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The synthesis scheme of CDs@CNC and sample photos are shown in Fig. 1 . The color of composite powder is light brown under daylight and bright green under UV irradiation (commercial 365 nm UV lamp).

The ion-doped CNC samples were synthesized to fabricate WLEDs. The preparation methods of the ion-doped CNC composites in this work refer to the previous reports and the doping ratio is based on the optimal value in references [20,24]. CDs were composited with the ion-doped CNC to verify the enhancement of CRI of the perovskite-based WLEDs after combination with CDs. The CDs@Cs2NaInCl6:0.01Sb&0.01Bi&1.2Mn samples were synthesized to prepare the cold WLEDs with high CRI. 0.01 mmol SbCl3 (0.002 g), 0.01 mmol BiCl3 (0.003 g), 1.2 mmol MnCl2 · 4H2O (0.237 g), 1 mmol InCl3 (0.221 g) and 1 mmol NaCl (0.059 g) were dissolved in 5 mL HCl with constant stirring and heated to 78 °C. The remaining synthesis steps were similar to that of the undopoed Cs2NaInCl6. The CDs@Cs2NaInCl6:00.1Bi&1.2Mn samples were synthesized to prepare the warm WLEDs with high CRI, and the steps of synthesis method were similar to that of Sb3+-Bi3+-Mn2+ doped Cs2NaInCl6, except that the SbCl3 was not added.

The surface morphology, element composition and distribution of the CDs@CNC composite materials were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and energy-dispersive spectroscope (EDS). The phases of CNC and composites were characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The microstructure of the CDs@CNC was analyzed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The Raman spectra of CDs and composites were characterized by a confocal Raman microscope with 75-mW laser at 488 nm. The infrared transmission spectrum of the CDs@CNC composite was characterized by a Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (FTIR). A carbon-sulfur analyzer was applied to test the loading content of CDs. The absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. A steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer was applied to measure the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and PL decay curves of the composites and precursor materials. The WLEDs were characterized by a spectra scan spectrophotometer with integrating sphere.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD patterns of Cs2NaInCl6 and CDs@CNC samples. The XRD pattern of CNC matches with reference [22] and the XRD pattern of CDs@CNC composite is highly similar to that of CNC, which indicates that the composite retains the cubic structure of the original CNC crystal. Here is no obvious XRD peak of CDs due to small amounts of CDs as well as the possible amorphous structure of synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), C, Cs, Na, In, and Cl elements were detected in the composite material by the EDS, where the C element can only come from CDs. The surface morphology and elemental distribution of composites were characterized by SEM and EDS mapping. The SEM image in Fig. 2 (c) shows the morphology of CDs@CNC composites and the image in Fig. S1 in Supporting Information (SI) shows the morphology of CNC hosts, indicating that CNC maintains the original octahedral morphology after the synthesis. Besides, the EDS mapping images in Fig. 2(d–g) show that the Cs, Na, and In elements are uniformly distributed in the visible octahedral shape. Fig. 2 (d) also shows a large amount of C element distributed outside octahedron due to the aggregation of uncombined CDs. However, the combined CDs cannot be clearly observed due to their dispersed distribution into the octahedron.

In order to directly observe the microstructure of nanoscale CDs in the composites, the CDs@CNC samples were analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the spherical CDs are well dispersed in the composite and the inset image shows an average particle size of 3.21 nm. Fig. 3 (b) shows the lattice fringes of CDs@CNC with an interplanar spacing of about 0.37 nm, corresponding to the (220) crystal planes of CNC crystals. Based on the previous reports [40,41], it can be typically observed that nanoparticles adhered to the surface exhibit distinct spherical profiles at the edges of the substrate. However, as shown in Fig. S2 (a) and (b) in SI, the CDs located at the periphery of CNC exhibit no evident spherical profile, which is similar to previous reports [42,43] about embedded CDs. Furthermore, Fig. S2 (c) and (d) in SI illustrate that there are a few CDs exhibiting a semi-spherical morphology, which is similar to the characteristics of semi-embedded nanoparticles [44,45]. These results indicate that the majority of CDs are embedded into the CNC crystals and a few CDs are semi embedded. Besides, there is no obvious lattice fringe of CDs, which confirms that the CDs in the composites are in the form of amorphous structure, consistent with the XRD result. In addition, Raman analysis is also an effective mean to characterize the fine structure of CDs, such as bonding. The Raman spectra of CDs and CDs@CNC samples are shown in Fig. 3 (c), and the D and G peaks can be observed for both samples, which are the main Raman features of carbon materials. The Raman peaks of composites are weak since they were affected by the fluorescence of embedded CDs. The pure CDs have much stronger Raman peaks due to the fluorescence quenching caused by aggregation [46]. The D peak at 1360 cm−1 represents the sp3 bonds or absence of carbon lattice while the G peak at 1580 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane vibration of carbon sp2 hybridization [47]. The integrated intensity ratio ID/IG for the D and G bands is widely used to reflect amorphization and graphitization of carbon materials. The value of ID/IG is 6.22 in this work, suggesting that the CDs in the composites are mainly amorphous carbon with a small amount of graphite-like structural carbon. Fig. 3 (d) shows the FT-IR spectrum of CDs@CNC composites to understand the surface functional groups. The stretching vibration peak at 3427 cm−1 indicates the presence of abundant –OH groups. The peak at 1630 cm−1 comes from the vibration of –CO groups, which confirms the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of composites. Besides, the peaks at 1588 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CC bonds and C–O–C bonds, respectively. The FT-IR results show that the surface of the composites contains a large number of organic groups introduced during the synthesis of CDs. Typically, according to the defect emission mechanism, the luminescence of CDs is caused by surface functional groups and surface structures of carbon [48]. Therefore, the organic functional groups in composites are necessary for embedded CDs to maintain their luminescence properties [34].

Based on the above characterization results, the CDs have been successfully embedded into the CNC crystals. The obtained CDs@CNC composites maintain the octahedral morphology of original CNC host and the characteristic functional groups of original CDs after combination, which ensures the application of CDs and double perovskite composite materials.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the absorption spectra of CDs@CNC composites and precursors (CDs solutions and pure CNC), respectively. Consistent with previous reports [49–51], the CDs solution shows a typical absorption peak at 270 nm corresponding to π–π* transitions and the absorption peaks at 328 nm and 404 nm attributed to n–π* transitions related to the edge of CDs core, which result in bright luminescence of the main emissive centers. The pure CNC has no absorption peak at the wavelength larger than 260 nm due to its wide band gap [20]. The absorption peaks of CDs@CNC are similar to those of CDs solutions in the ultraviolet band, which indicates that the CDs in the composites retain good optical absorption. In order to verify the applicability of composites with commercial UV chips, 365 nm as the characteristic wavelength of the commercial UV lamp used in Fig. 1 is selected as the excitation wavelength to obtain the PL emission spectra of CDs@CNC composites, as shown in Fig. S3 in SI. A strong emission peak at about 485 nm is observed in all the composite samples. Furthermore, in order to find the most efficient excitation wavelength, the PL excitation (PLE) spectrum of composites is characterized, as shown in Fig. 4 (b), where two PLE peaks at 287 nm and 396 nm are observed. Both PLE peaks well match the emission of commercial UV chips, indicating the application feasibility of composites in UV excited phosphors. Since the PLE peak at 396 nm is stronger, thus it is selected as the excitation wavelength to obtain a high intensity fluorescence emission. Fig. 4 (b) shows the PL spectra of CDs@CNC composites and CDs solutions, where two strong emission peaks at 489 nm and 531 nm are observed, corresponding to the cyan-green emission of CDs@CNC composites and the green emission of CDs solutions, respectively. The structure of CDs is related to their fluorescence properties, and the abundance of sp3 carbon structure is important for green light emission [38,52]. The emission spectrum of pure CNC is not given in Fig. 4 (b) because it has no obvious emission peak, consistent with the previous reports [53,54]. The results show that the composites generate green light emission with UV excitation due to the introduction of CDs. The green emission peak of composites slightly red-shifts with the increasing excitation wavelength, similar to CDs solutions, as shown in Fig. S4 in SI, indicating that the CDs maintain the wavelength dependent PL after combination. Due to the fact that CDs often consist of multiple emission centers, the excitation dependence is attributed to their different structure types and surface functional groups [55,56]. As reported in Ref. [30], the precursor content is related to the luminescence property of the final products, so the PL spectra of composites prepared with different CDs contents were analyzed. Based on the previous reports, the loading content of CDs was determined by recording the weight difference [57] and testing with a carbon-sulfur analyzer [58]. As shown in Table S1 in SI, the weights of the samples loaded with different amounts of CDs were recorded. The samples were additionally tested by a carbon-sulfur analyzer and the results are shown in Table S2 in SI. Based on the above results, the loading content of CDs increases with the increase of CDs content in the precursor. As shown in Fig. S5 in SI, the PL intensity firstly increases and then decreases with the increasing content of CDs content. At the lower CDs concentration, the increased number of emission centers in the composites enhances the PL intensity due to the introduction of CDs. However, with the further increase of CDs content, the aggregation of excessive CDs quenches the fluorescence of the composites. The CDs@CNC composite prepared from the CDs solutions with concentration of 22.5 mg/mL exhibits best fluorescence performance in this work, which is chosen for subsequent analysis and synthesis of ion doped composites.

The fluorescence spectrum of CDs@CNC in Fig. 4 (b) shows a blue-shift compared to CDs solutions and its full width at half maximum (88 nm) is narrower than that of CDs solutions (106 nm). According to previous studies [59–61], the surface state of CDs plays a dominant role in their optical properties. The difference of PL spectra between CDs and composites may be attributed to the changes of emission centers and surface defect states. In general, the resonant energy transfer and energy state recombination of coupled surface defects induced by self-aggregation result in the redshift and broadening of emission bands of CDs [62]. Therefore, the blueshift and narrowing of the emission band in Fig. 4 (b) cause less energy loss and less surface defect states in the CDs@CNC composites [34,63]. The subsequent results of fluorescence lifetime of CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites confirm this since the resonant energy transfer is competitive with fluorescence lifetime.

The PL decay curves of CDs solutions and composites are shown in Fig. 5 and the collected luminescence attenuation curves can be fitted to the following equation [64,65]:
(1)
R
( t )
=
B 1
e
(
− t
τ 1
)
+
B 2
e
(
− t
τ 2
)
where τ1 and τ2 represent the times corresponding to different decay processes; B1 and B2 are the weighted values of short decay process and long decay process, respectively. The carriers are easy to be trapped by the surface domains, which will lead to the long lifetime radiation, so the long decay lifetime comes from surface-related luminescence [66,67]. The short decay lifetime is attributed to the intrinsic radiation of graphitized cores [68]. The fitting parameters of PL decay curves of CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites are shown in Table S3 and the average lifetime is calculated from the equation [33]:
(2)
τ
a v g
=
∑
i = 1
n
B i
τ i

The calculated average decay time of CDs increases from 5.49 ns to 8.95 ns after combination with CNC, indicating that the resonance energy transfer is weakened and the radiative transition is more probable, which leads to the blue-shift and broadening of the CDs emission band. In addition, the PLQY of CDs increases from 7.21% to 14.89% after combination, which is probably due to the less energy loss and protection of CNC host [69]. In general, the fluorescence performance of CDs aqueous solutions is affected by proton transfer due to the proton solvent property of water [33,70]. However, the proton transfer does not occur because of the absence of water in the solid CDs dispersed CNC host. Therefore, the PLQY of the composites is obviously improved in this work.

Based on the characterization of optical properties, the CDs@CNC composites exhibit optical absorption in the UV waveband and green light emission, which satisfies the application requirement of green light phosphor excited by commercial UV chips and could be potential candidate to obtain green light in CNC based phosphors without rare earth elements.

The stability is very important to evaluate the suitability of CDs@CNC composites for practical WLEDs applications, so the effects of UV irradiation, heat, and air on the optical properties of original CDs and CDs@CNC composites are systematically studied. In order to avoid the fluorescence quenching caused by aggregation, the stability of the CDs was tested by dispersing them in an aqueous solution. Firstly, the resistance of samples to UV irradiation was tested by the continuous exposure to UV light (1.88 W/cm2) from a portable UV lamp. Fig. 6 (a) shows the PL intensity variation of pure CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites at different irradiation durations. The fluorescence intensity of pure CDs solutions decreased by nearly half due to the photobleaching effect [71]. Nevertheless, the composites exhibit better stability for UV irradiation and 90% of original PL intensity is retained after 240 min irradiation. The 10% reduction in fluorescence intensity may be attributed to the photooxidation as observed in conventional lead-based halides [72–74]. The photooxidation degradation process generally includes the following main steps: generation of superoxide species on the surface under UV light irradiation, fast oxidation of the surface and production of water, slow hydration of the inner lead-based halides, and final destruction of the materials lattices [75]. In order to prove the thermal stability of composites against the heat generated by WLED during operation, the pure CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites were heated to 100 °C and then naturally cooled to room temperature for 7 cycles. After each cycle, the PL intensity is tested and the data are shown in Fig. 6 (b). The PL intensity of pure CDs solutions shows 7% reduction while that of the CDs@CNC is almost unchanged after 7 heating-cooling cycles, indicating that the composites possess better thermal stability. Finally, the pure CDs solutions and CDs@CNC composites were kept in air atmosphere (18–22 °C and 50–80% humidity) for 15 days and the PL intensity is also tested at 3-day interval. As shown in Fig. 6 (c) and (d), the PL intensity of CDs solutions exhibits obvious degradation while that of the composites hardly change. The XRD patterns of composites on Day 1 and Day 15 shown in Fig. S6 in SI are almost same, indicating the unchanged crystal structure of composites in air, consistent with the constant PL intensity. Besides, Fig. S7 in SI shows that there is no shift observed for the emission peaks of composites, which further confirms the good stability of CDs@CNC composites for UV irradiation, heat, and air owing to the protection of CNC host [34]. The good stability ensures the potential application of CDs@CNC composites in WLEDs.

To demonstrate the potential application of multicolor emission of CDs@CNC based phosphors, the ion doped composites, CDs@CNC:Bi3+,Mn3+ and CDs@CNC: Sb3+,Bi3+,Mn2+ were synthesized to obtain multicolor phosphor and the corresponding WLED devices were fabricated by coating composites on commercial UV chips with different wavebands (280–290 nm, 360–370 nm, 370–380 nm). Fig. 7 shows the PL spectra of ion-doped composites and the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of the WLED devices. The insets are the photos of devices with cold white, quasi white, and warm white emissions, respectively. In detail, Fig. 7(a) shows the cold white emission with CIE color coordinate (0.2751, 0.2749) of CDs@CNC:Sb,Bi,Mn composites at 373 nm excitation. The CDs@CNC:Bi,Mn composites exhibit quasi-white emission with 5312 K color temperature at 360 nm excitation as shown in Fig. 7(b) and its CIE chromaticity coordinate (0.3365, 0.3334) highly matches with pure white light (0.33, 0.33) [76]. In Fig. 7(c), the CDs@CNC:Bi,Mn at 285 nm excitation exhibits warm white light with a low color temperature (3957K) and CIE chromaticity coordinate (0.3795, 0.3658). The optical performance parameters of the three devices with different color temperatures are listed in Table 1 and their CRI values are greater than 80. As shown in Fig. S8 in SI, the CRI and luminous efficiency of WLEDs generally remain constant with the increase of currents. However, WLEDs exhibit unsatisfactory luminous efficiency, which is attributed to the high energy consumption of the UV chips [77]. The multicolor emissions originated from CDs and ion-doped CNC are blue (450 nm), green (489), yellow (570 nm), and red (620 nm), which are attributed to the Sb3+, CDs, Bi3+ and Mn2+, respectively [20,23,24]. In order to avoid the redundant blue light caused by the blue-shift of CDs emission after combination, the yellow light was introduced by Bi3+ doping as the complementary color to blue light. Additionally, the emission of multiple colors contributes to a higher CRI [74]. Fig. 8 illustrates the influence of CDs on CRI and PL spectra, where the CRI value increases significantly from 66.24 (CNC:Sb,Bi,Mn) to 90.95 (CDs@CNC:Sb,Bi,Mn) because the green emission band of CNC:Sb,Bi,Mn located in 490–560 nm is filled with the CDs. Moreover, Fig. S9 in SI shows the PL spectra and the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs@CNC:Bi,Mn composites at the different excitation wavelengths (270–365 nm). The relative intensity of green, yellow, and red emissions changes with the varied excitation wavelength, implying that the cold-warm white fluorescence can be flexibly tuned. These results indicate that rare-earth-free CNC based phosphors can emit green light by combination with CDs and the CRI of the corresponding WLED devices is effectively improved. Therefore, the CDs@CNC composites could be a promising candidate as white light emission phosphors toward the next generation high-quality lighting devices. The combination method presented in this work is applicable to other double perovskite materials.

The carbon quantum dots are uniformly incorporated in the double perovskite Cs2NaInCl6 host and the solid-state CDs retain their original optical properties. As a result, the prepared rare-earth-free CDs@CNC composites exhibit green emission with good stability for UV irradiation, heat, and air. The WLED devices based on ion-doped CNC possess the tunable white light emission with high CRI up to 90. This work provides a new route to design the next generation high-quality lighting devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12275047).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2023.06.133.
